questions.specification:
A design firm is launching a new 3D-printed accessory. The annual fixed costs for the production facility are calculated at $$\$4,800$$. The variable cost per unit is $$\$12$$, and the retail selling price is set at $$\$20$$. 

What is the break-even point for this product?

Koru is a start-up designing a compact, foldable bicycle helmet for commuters, see Figure 1. The team is deciding whether the product is economically viable and how to price it for launch.

![Figure 1: A compact foldable commuter helmet on a desk](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/ab095c6c-f685-4087-a193-2e3d4cb891b0.png)

The following estimates are available:

- Fixed costs for tooling, certification, and launch campaign: USD 180,000
- Variable cost per helmet (materials, assembly, packaging): USD 24
- Retailers require a margin of USD 40 on the retail selling price (RSP)
- Koru is considering selling direct-to-consumer (DTC) online as well as through retailers

parts.content: Outline one way a designer could reduce the variable cost per helmet without compromising perceived quality.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute a lower-cost material/finish that maintains required performance/specification (e.g., same impact rating) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Simplify geometry/part count to reduce manufacturing or assembly time while retaining key user-facing features (comfort/fit/aesthetics) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline how using break-even analysis would help Koru judge whether launching the helmet is economically viable.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify the break-even quantity/point where total revenue equals total costs <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use the break-even figure to assess whether forecast demand/capacity is sufficient (and therefore whether the launch is likely viable) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain one advantage and one disadvantage of Koru using a penetration pricing strategy when selling the helmet direct-to-consumer online.
parts.markscheme:
- Advantage: Lower initial price can increase adoption/market share quickly (reduces perceived risk, encourages trial, builds reviews/word of mouth) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Advantage linked to viability: Higher early sales volume can spread fixed costs, moving toward break-even sooner <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Disadvantage: Lower margins may make it harder to recover fixed costs/fund support and future development <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Disadvantage: May anchor customers’ reference price and make later price increases difficult <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Disadvantage: Can trigger price competition, reducing profitability across channels <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Clear explanation applied to the DTC context (e.g., online transparency/price comparison, acquisition costs) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Max 6 marks</note>

questions.specification:
An industrial design company is planning the production run for a new ergonomic chair. The economic viability is assessed using the following data:

| Financial Category | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Monthly Fixed Costs | $40,000$ |
| Variable Costs (per unit) | $120$ |
| Wholesale Unit Price | $200$ |

How many units must the company sell each month to reach the break-even point?

**Aurora Audio** is preparing to launch a set of modular, repairable wireless earbuds aimed at commuters. The product uses a charging case, two earbuds, and replaceable parts (battery module, speaker driver, and silicone tips). Aurora Audio is deciding whether the first production run should be made locally (higher labour cost, lower shipping cost) or overseas (lower labour cost, higher shipping cost and longer lead time).

Aurora Audio has estimated the following **unit costs** for the local production option (per set of earbuds):

| Cost item | Cost per unit (USD) |
|---|---:|
| Electronic components (drivers, chips, battery cells) | 18.50 |
| Plastics/metal parts and finishing | 6.20 |
| Assembly labour | 9.80 |
| Quality testing and returns allowance | 2.00 |
| Packaging | 1.50 |
| Shipping to warehouse | 1.20 |
| Variable warranty provision | 1.80 |

In addition, Aurora Audio expects these **fixed costs** for the launch year:

| Fixed cost item | Annual cost (USD) |
|---|---:|
| Tooling for injection moulds and jigs | 120,000 |
| Product certification and compliance testing | 35,000 |
| Website, photography and launch marketing | 85,000 |
| Salaries (2 staff) | 110,000 |
| Rent and utilities | 50,000 |

Aurora Audio plans to sell through:
- its own website (payment fees and fulfilment add **$4.00 per unit**), and
- a retail partner who requires a **40% margin on the retail price**.

Figure 1 shows the earbuds and charging case.

![Photo of modular wireless earbuds next to an open charging case, with a small screwdriver and a replacement battery module indicating repairability.](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/notes/67300c53-2313-4741-ac8b-a6c9e8085131.png)

parts.content: Identify two different types of costs Aurora Audio must consider when designing and launching the earbuds, giving an example for each.
parts.markscheme:
- Identifies a valid cost type (e.g., fixed, variable, direct, indirect/overhead, one-off/non-recurring) and gives a correct example from the case study that matches the identified type <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Identifies a second, different valid cost type and gives a correct example from the case study that matches the second type <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
Aurora Audio is considering a pricing approach that supports its brand message of repairability and long service life. 

Outline how a pricing strategy could communicate value rather than simply being the lowest price.
parts.markscheme:
- Outlines a suitable strategy linked to value (e.g., value-based pricing, premium pricing, price skimming at launch, bundling spares/extended warranty, subscription for replacement parts) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Links the strategy to communicating value/brand proposition (e.g., signals quality, longevity, total cost of ownership, repairability) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: List two factors from the case study that would affect the final price Aurora Audio sets in each sales channel.
parts.markscheme:
- Correct factor affecting price (e.g., retailer 40% margin requirement; website payment/fulfilment $4 per unit; different customer willingness to pay; channel costs; returns/warranty provision; competitive positioning) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Second correct factor affecting price <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how Aurora Audio could use cost information to set a viable retail price, taking into account both unit costs and channel requirements.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculates or states an appropriate unit variable cost from the table (e.g., sums variable production costs) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Recognises additional per-unit channel costs (e.g., adds $4 for website fulfilment/payment if relevant) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains contribution margin concept (price must exceed variable cost to contribute to fixed costs/profit) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Correctly applies retail margin requirement (40% margin implies wholesale price is 60% of retail price) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Links outcome to ensuring sufficient margin/contribution to cover fixed costs and desired profit <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate three ways Aurora Audio could increase the likelihood of breaking even or making a profit, using information from the case study.
parts.markscheme:
- Proposes and explains a valid action to improve viability (e.g., reduce unit costs via design-for-manufacture, supplier negotiation, simplify parts/finishes, reduce packaging/testing waste) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Shows how the action affects contribution margin/break-even/profit (e.g., lowers variable cost or increases effective price) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Proposes and explains a second valid action (e.g., adjust channel mix toward direct-to-consumer to avoid retailer margin, introduce bundles/accessories, staged rollout to reduce fixed marketing spend) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Shows how the second action affects contribution/break-even/profit <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Proposes and explains a third valid action (e.g., pricing tactics: skimming then planned reductions, penetration for volume, tiered product line, extended warranty/parts revenue; reduce fixed costs via leasing tooling, delaying hires) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Shows how the third action affects contribution/break-even/profit <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Includes a reasoned judgement about viability that references fixed vs variable costs and/or channel constraints <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Uses case-study data appropriately to support evaluation (any correct quantitative or qualitative reference) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Considers a limitation/risk (e.g., demand uncertainty, returns rate, price sensitivity, lead time) when evaluating viability <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification:
A technology startup is assessing the economic viability of a new robotic educational kit. The following financial projections have been established for the first year of commercial production:

| Financial Variable | Projection (USD) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Total Fixed Costs (tooling, rent, and insurance) | $$120,000$$ |
| Variable Cost per unit (materials and direct labor) | $$45$$ |
| Expected Wholesale Selling Price per unit | $$105$$ |

What is the minimum number of units the company must sell to reach the break-even point?

A start-up called **AquaPulse** is preparing to launch a smart, reusable water bottle that tracks hydration and syncs with a phone app. Customers can configure the product online by choosing materials and add-ons.

The design team has gathered the following cost information for the first production run.

**Costs per unit**

| Item | Cost per unit |
|---|---:|
| Stainless-steel bottle body | $6.40 |
| Lid + gasket | $1.10 |
| Sensor module | $8.90 |
| Packaging | $0.80 |
| Assembly labour (12 minutes at $18/hour) | $3.60 |

**Monthly fixed costs**

| Item | Monthly cost |
|---|---:|
| Rent and utilities | $3,200 |
| Salaries (2 staff) | $7,600 |
| Insurance and licenses | $900 |
| Website and app hosting | $650 |

AquaPulse is deciding between two pricing strategies:
- **Strategy A (cost-plus):** add a 40% markup to the unit cost.
- **Strategy B (penetration):** set a low launch price of $24.99 for the first 3 months to build market share.

The team forecasts demand of **1,200 units per month** at $24.99, but only **800 units per month** if priced above $30.

parts.content: Identify two examples of **variable costs** for AquaPulse from the information provided.
parts.markscheme:
- Names one correct variable cost item from the per-unit list (e.g., bottle body, lid + gasket, sensor module, packaging, assembly labour) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Names a second correct variable cost item from the per-unit list <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Outline two ways the online configurator could support a **pricing strategy** that improves perceived value for customers.
parts.markscheme:
- Explains that showing a live total/transparent price breakdown helps customers see what they are paying for and choose options within budget (supports value-based/customisation pricing) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains that add-ons/tiers (e.g., engraving, warranty) enable price discrimination/upselling by letting different customers pay different prices for different bundles <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using the cost data, calculate the **break-even quantity per month** if AquaPulse chooses Strategy B ($24.99).
parts.markscheme:
- Calculates variable cost per unit: $6.40+$1.10+$8.90+$0.80+$3.60=$20.80 <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Calculates total monthly fixed costs: $3,200+$7,600+$900+$650=$12,350 <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Calculates contribution per unit: $24.99-$20.80=$4.19 (accept $4.19 or $4.190) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Break-even quantity: $12,350\div 4.19\approx 2,948$ units (accept correct rounding, e.g., $2,947$–$2,949$) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate whether Strategy A or Strategy B is more economically viable for the first 3 months. Use evidence from the data.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculates/uses Strategy A price: $20.80\times 1.40=$29.12 (or explains cost-plus pricing result is below $30) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Uses demand evidence to compare likely sales volumes (e.g., $1,200$/month at $24.99 vs $800$/month above $30; notes Strategy A at $29.12 may avoid the “above $30” drop but still differs from penetration intent) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Compares monthly profit contributions using contribution and fixed costs (e.g., Strategy B contribution $4.19$; at $1,200$ units contribution $\approx 5,028$ so does not cover fixed costs $12,350$ → loss) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- For Strategy A, computes contribution per unit: $29.12-20.80=$8.32 and estimates outcome at a justified volume (e.g., if $800$ units, contribution $6,656$ still below fixed costs; if $1,200$ units, contribution $9,984$ still below fixed costs) and concludes which is closer to break-even and why <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Makes a justified recommendation referencing economic viability (e.g., neither breaks even at forecast volumes; Strategy A improves contribution and reduces losses, while Strategy B may be justified only if it increases future demand/retention beyond 3 months) <marks>1 mark</marks>

questions.specification: A startup enterprise is assessing the financial feasibility of a new biodegradable container. The tooling and setup costs are fixed at $$45,000$$, while materials and labor represent a variable cost of $$2.50$$ per unit. If each container is sold for $$7.00$$, what is the minimum number of units that must be sold to reach the break-even point?
